coredata.dc:description: The exploration of pure organic ultra-long room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials has emerged as a research hotspot in recent years. Herein, a simple strategy for fabricating long-afterglow polymer aerogels with three-dimensional ordered structures and environmental monitoring capabilities is proposed. Based on the non-covalent interactions between pectin (PC) and melamine formaldehyde (MF), a composite aerogel (PCMF@phenanthrene) (PCMF@PA) doped with phosphorescent organic small molecules was constructed. It exhibits a stable and persistent afterglow, with a phosphorescence lifetime reaching up to 1.99 s. Simultaneously, this aerogel possesses excellent mechanical properties, having a compressive modulus of 4.14 MPa, which is 490.8 times that of the PC aerogel. Its friction coefficient is also much lower than that of the single MF aerogel, enabling the material to achieve a better balance between rigidity and service life in practical applications. Moreover, through resonance energy transfer, the afterglow wavelength was redshifted from 504 nm to 576 nm and 620 nm, and aerogels with ultra-long yellow and red afterglows were successfully obtained. PCMF@PA aerogels display specific chemical stability in different organic solvents. Notably, PCMF@PA has a characteristic recognition for formic acid gas. The change in the luminous intensity and lifetime of the aerogel after gas absorption distinguishes it from gases such as ammonia and acetic acid. These phosphorescent polymer aerogels with self-monitoring and tracing capabilities not only foster the advancement of ordered phosphorescent materials but also broaden the application scope of RTP materials in environmental monitoring.

head.ce:title: Gas sensing by long-wavelength and long-afterglow pectin/melamine-formaldehyde aerogel via resonance energy transfer

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Sciences, University of Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing 101408 China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Zeyu Gao and Tengyang Cao contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The exploration of pure organic ultra-long room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials has emerged as a research hotspot in recent years. Herein, a simple strategy for fabricating long-afterglow polymer aerogels with three-dimensional ordered structures and environmental monitoring capabilities is proposed. Based on the non-covalent interactions between pectin (PC) and melamine formaldehyde (MF), a composite aerogel (PCMF@phenanthrene) (PCMF@PA) doped with phosphorescent organic small molecules was constructed. It exhibits a stable and persistent afterglow, with a phosphorescence lifetime reaching up to 1.99 s. Simultaneously, this aerogel possesses excellent mechanical properties, having a compressive modulus of 4.14 MPa, which is 490.8 times that of the PC aerogel. Its friction coefficient is also much lower than that of the single MF aerogel, enabling the material to achieve a better balance between rigidity and service life in practical applications. Moreover, through resonance energy transfer, the afterglow wavelength was redshifted from 504 nm to 576 nm and 620 nm, and aerogels with ultra-long yellow and red afterglows were successfully obtained. PCMF@PA aerogels display specific chemical stability in different organic solvents. Notably, PCMF@PA has a characteristic recognition for formic acid gas. The change in the luminous intensity and lifetime of the aerogel after gas absorption distinguishes it from gases such as ammonia and acetic acid. These phosphorescent polymer aerogels with self-monitoring and tracing capabilities not only foster the advancement of ordered phosphorescent materials but also broaden the application scope of RTP materials in environmental monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Recently, pure organic ultra-long room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have attracted extensive attention due to their unique photophysical properties [1–8]. However, most materials with shorter-wavelength phosphorescence tend to exhibit longer afterglow durations, while those with longer-wavelength emissions typically have shorter lifetimes. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), which transfers excitons from the singlet (S1) and triplet (T1) states of the donor (host) to the singlet state of the acceptor (guest), has been validated as an efficient method for achieving full-color luminescent material systems [9–12]. Drawing inspiration from FRET's strategy for enhancing full-color luminescence, introducing T1-S1 phosphorescent FRET into the host–guest system, with a suitable RTP substrate as the energy donor and a suitable luminescent object as the energy acceptor, it is possible to effectively regulate the color of the afterglow to obtain a long-wavelength material with long-afterglow. In addition, polymer-based RTP materials can protect phosphorescent molecules from oxygen in a variety of ways, thereby inhibiting non-radiative transitions of triplet excitons [13–17]. Covalent bonds or supramolecular interactions between polymers and phosphorescent molecules can also inhibit the vibration and rotation of phosphorescent molecules and reduce the quenching of triplet excitons, allowing the triplet excitons to exist long enough to emit phosphorescence [18,19]. Therefore, these properties render the polymer an ideal candidate for long-lived RTP materials. Furthermore, polymer-based RTP materials have aroused great interest for innovative applications in the fields of information security [20,21], sensors [22,23], and organic light-emitting diodes [24] owing to their inherent advantages such as low cost, high machinability and good biocompatibility.

At present, RTP materials have been widely studied, among which the macroscopic morphology of solid phase RTP materials is mainly powder and film. Limited by their respective physical morphology, they are widely used in optoelectronic parts and anti-counterfeiting fields. Endowing phosphorescent materials with a three-dimensional (3D) form could significantly expand their practical applications. Aerogel, a type of 3D solid materials with a highly interpenetrating structure, possesses characteristics such as low density, high porosity and surface area, absence of secondary pollution, and ease of recovery. These properties have led to their widespread use in applications like drug delivery [25–28], packaging [29–31], thermal insulation [32–35], energy storage [36–39], catalysis [40–42], and adsorption [43–52]. Fluorescent aerogels represented by silica, graphene, and polymers have become effective materials for detecting metal ion-containing wastewater and harmful gases [52–55]. Among them, fluorescent polymer aerogels have attracted great attention due to their low cost, simple preparation, and high machinability. Presently, a variety of fluorescent polymer aerogels like cellulose and chitosan have been developed for the detection of waste water and harmful gases [56,57]. Compared with the former, phosphorescent polymer aerogels have not been extensively studied. Therefore, it is urgent to develop phosphorescent aerogels which can be applied for environmental purification monitoring. Due to the quenching of phosphorescence caused by water and oxygen, exploring the sensitivity of RTP materials to the environment has become an important direction, which also provides a new idea for RTP sensors.

Pectin (PC) is a diversified linear polysaccharide that exists in plant cell walls and fruits, in which the linear chain composed of galacturonic acid units contains rich carboxyl and hydroxyl groups. It is not only low-cost, renewable, and biocompatible, but also has a particularly stable gelatinization ability. Compared with pectin hydrogels, aerogels have many advantages such as high porosity, large specific surface area, good adsorption capacity, and easy recovery. Currently, pectin aerogels are used in drug delivery [58–64], antibacterial [65], adsorption [66–70], and capacitors [71]. However, the mechanical properties and water resistance of pure pectin aerogel are poor, so it cannot be independently constructed to RTP aerogel with good comprehensive properties. Melamine formaldehyde (MF) is typical thermosetting materials with high mechanical strength and excellent resistance to water, heat, and chemical corrosion. MF enriched with nitrogen atoms and hydrogen-bonded donors not only exhibit excitation- clustering-triggered emission (CTE) effects, but also, with the shortening of the spacing of the triazine moieties, tends to rigidify the molecular chain conformation and further increases the degree of clustering, resulting in enhanced fluorescence accompanied by long-lasting phosphorescence (>100 ms). More importantly, the introduction of phosphorescent small-molecule guests into MF polymer matrix can conveniently realize ultralong-lasting room-temperature phosphorescence via cross-linking [72]. Recently, we successfully constructed a multicolor phosphorescent composite aerogel temperature sensor with excellent fire safety and favorable mechanical properties based on the ordered interpenetrating structure of polyvinyl alcohol and melamine formaldehyde doped with organic phosphorescent small molecules. The prepared aerogels doped with phosphorescent molecules also have excellent water-resistant stability and can remain bright after 4 weeks of immersion in water [73].

In this paper, in order to realize the application of PC aerogel in the direction of RTP environmental monitoring, as well as to solve the problem of single color at short wavelength of RTP, flexible water-sensitive pectin is first combined with rigid MF. Directional freeze-drying was used to construct polymer aerogels with long-lived phosphorescence based on non-covalent connection between PC and MF doped with luminescent molecules. On this basis, a long-wavelength polymer aerogel with long afterglow was constructed by FRET using a long-lived phosphorescent aerogel as energy donor and a luminescent molecule as energy accepter. Finally, the sensitivity of long-afterglow polymer aerogel to solvent and the changes of phosphorescence intensity and lifetime after gas absorption were investigated. This study broadens the application field of phosphorescent materials as promising ordered 3D polymer aerogel in environmental monitoring.

Pectin (PC, galacturonic acid ≥74.0 %, DE = 60 %, Mw = 160 kDa), melamine, formaldehyde (37 wt% aqueous solution), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), 2-methyltetrahydrofuran (2-MeTHF), Phenanthrene (PA), sulforhodamine 101 (SR101), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), and ethyl acetate (EA) were obtained from Macklin (Shanghai, China). p-Toluenesulfonic acid was purchased from OKA (Beijing, China). Rhodamine 6G (R6G) was purchased from Bailingwei (Beijing, China). Toluene (Tol), diethyl ether (Et2O), dichloromethane (DCM), and acetone (ACE) were purchased from Tongguang (Beijing, China). Ethanol (EtOH) and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Mreda (Beijing, China). Formic acid, ammonia water, and Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO) purchased from Meryer (Shanghai, China). All ingredients were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PC aqueous solution and MF prepolymer
ce:section.ce:para: An aqueous solution of PC was prepared by dissolving a certain mass (1 g or 3 g) of PC in 100 mL of deionized water (DI) water at 60 °C and stirring for 3 h. 6 g melamine and 10 g formaldehyde solution (37 wt%) were added to 10 mL deionized water. Adjust the pH of the mixed solution to 8.0–9.0 with 0.1 M NaOH solution. The mixture was stirred at 90 °C with a magnetic stirrer. After a few minutes, the melamine was completely dissolved in the formaldehyde solution, and the solution became transparent. Stirring was continued for 30 min to obtain an MF prepolymer with a mass fraction of 37.5 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PCMF composite aerogel, pure PC aerogel and pure MF aerogel
ce:section.ce:para: PC and MF were mixed in mass ratios of 1:16 and 1:5.3. The mixture was adjusted to about pH 6.0 by means of a p-toluene sulfonic acid solution. The mixed solution was then poured into the mold and immediately subjected to directional freezing with a homemade liquid nitrogen device, freeze-dried at −50 °C and less than 1 Pa for 48 h (LGJ-10 Vacuum Freeze-dryer, China). Subsequently, the freeze-dried PCMF aerogels were cross-linked in a vacuum oven at 80 °C and under pressure of less than 1 Pa for 1 day. The pure PC and pure MF aerogels were prepared by the same method (Scheme 1 ). For convenience, the direction of ice crystal growth was defined as radial, and the direction perpendicular to it was defined as tangential. The name of the aerogels were indicated by the quality of the pectin, and the sample with a PC mass of 1 g and 3 g were termed named PCMF-1 and PCMF-3, respectively (Table S1).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of PCMF composite aerogel doped with luminescent molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Dissolve 2 mg of phenanthrene in 0.2 mL of 2-MeTHF, and dissolve 2 mg of R6G and SR101 in 0.2 mL of water, respectively. The MF prepolymer was cooled to 70 °C, the PA solution was added, and the mixture was stirred under a magnetic stirrer for 20 min. The MF prepolymer with phosphorescent molecules was mixed with PC solution, and the mixture was adjusted to about pH 6.0 by p-toluene sulfonic acid solution. Then, the mixture was poured into molds and immediately subjected to directional freezing with a homemade liquid nitrogen device. The samples were freeze-dried and cross-linked to the above process to obtain the doped aerogels PCMF@PA. R6G, SR101, PA&R6G, PA&SR101, and PA&R6G&SR101 were mixed into MF prepolymer by the same method, and PCMF@R6G, PCMF@SR101, PCMF@PG, PCMF@PS, and PCMF@PGS were obtained. For comparison, PA is added to PC and MF to get PC@PA and MF@PA, respectively.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy of the sample was performed in the attenuated total reflection (ATR) mode over a VERTEX 70 (BRUKER, Germany) with wave numbers ranging from 4000 to 400 cm−1. Thermogravimetry and differential scanning calorimetry measurements were also collected. Thermal stability was measured by thermogravimetry–differential scanning calorimetry on a synchronous thermal analyzer (PerkinElmer STA6000, USA). About 2 mg of the sample was placed in an aluminum dish and heated from room temperature to 600 °C under N2 atmosphere at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The density of the aerogel was calculated by measuring its mass with an electronic analytical balance and its size using a vernier caliper. The morphological microstructure of the aerogel was characterized using a Regulus 8220 scanning electron microscope at an accelerated voltage of 3 kV. Prior to testing, the observed cross sections were prepared by fracturing the sample in liquid nitrogen, and gold was coated with a sputtering coater for observation by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The porosity and pore size were measured using the mercury intrusion method with a high-performance automatic mercury injection instrument (Micromeritics AutoPore V 9620, USA). The friction coefficient of aerogel was tested by friction and wear testing machine (UMT-2, USA). The test condition was that the loading force was 2 N, the friction pair was silicon nitride ceramic ball with diameter of 6 mm, and the circular motion was 50 r/min. The creep test was carried out with rheometer (DHR-2, USA) under the test condition of 25 °C, load of 10 N and external force of 3000 Pa. Photoluminescence spectra and lifetime tests were collected using a steady-state transient fluorescence spectrometer (FLS1000, UK). Compression tests were performed using an electronic universal testing machine (Instron 5967, USA) with a 30 KN load cell and a 2 mm/min pressure disc test rate. Four samples of each aerogel with dimensions of approximately 20 × 20 × 20 mm were tested in parallel.

ce:section.ce:section-title: PCMF Aerogel: from preparation to mechanical and thermal attributes
Preparation and characterization of PCMF aerogel
ce:section.ce:para: The preparation of the PCMF composite aerogel includes three steps: chemical crosslinking, freeze-drying, and aging (Scheme 1). To investigate the effect of the relative contents of PC and MF on the properties of the aerogel, two PCMF aerogels with different proportions, PCMF-1 and PCMF-3, were prepared. For comparison, pure PC and pure MF aerogel were simultaneously prepared by the same method. The molar ratio of melamine to formaldehyde in the PCMF composite aerogel was the same as 1:2.5 in previous work [73]. The obtained aerogel has a more complete network structure at this ratio.

The PC, MF, PCMF-1, and PCMF-3 aerogels were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) (Fig. 1 a). The strong absorption peak of PC aerogel at 3372 cm−1 represents the stretching vibration of secondary hydroxyl group. The sharp absorption peak at 1742 cm−1 is the stretching vibration of the CO bond. In contrast, MF aerogel absorption peaks are almost invisible in these two places. The combined PCMF-1 and PCMF-3 aerogels retain the absorption peaks of secondary hydroxyl group and CO bonds, while their strength is lower than that of PC. At the same time, the absorption peaks of PCMF-1 and PCMF-3 aerogel at 809 cm−1 and 1490 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibration of the triazine torus in MF. The absorption peak at 1555 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption peak of triazine. The absorption peaks at 1342 cm−1 and 995 cm−1 are the bending vibration peaks of CN bond and OH, respectively. The characteristic peak at 3320 cm−1 corresponds to the NH stretching vibration peak of MF [65,72,74]. These results indicate that PC and MF components are presented in PCMF aerogel.

The visual microstructure of aerogel was observed by SEM. PC solution forms ice crystals due to a large amount of water during the directional freezing process, inducing PC to form a layered structure along the direction of ice crystal growth. The aerogel had a directional pore structure with a pore size of about 20 μm (Fig. 1b). For 37.5 wt% MF prepolymer solution, due to its high concentration and intrinsic random properties, the resulting aerogel presents a uniform 3D cell network structure with a pore size of about 5 μm and a thin wall between the pores (about 3 μm, Fig. 1b), even after directional freezing. At the same time, it can be observed on SEM that although the composite aerogel maintains the directional pore structure formed by PC along directional freezing on the whole, the pore diameter and pore wall thickness are reduced, and the 3D network structure on the thin wall is more uniform. This is due to the interpenetrating cross-linked network formed between hydroxymethylated melamine and PC in the composite aerogel, and the synergistic effect of rich hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces between MF and PC in the network. Due to the similar mass and density of PCMF-1 and PCMF-3, the pore sizes are both about 20 μm.

On this basis, the porosity (Table 1 ) and pore size (Fig. 1c) of the aerogel were measured using the mercury intrusion method. PC and MF aerogel are soft and brittle, respectively, which are easy to deform and collapse during mercury intrusion, so useful data cannot be obtained. In the process of mercury intrusion, the soft PC is easy to deform and the brittle MF is easy to collapse, giving no meaningful data. The porosity of PCMF-1 and PCMF-3 composite aerogel is about 89 %, which corresponds to their microstructure.

Under the environmental conditions with a temperature of 26 °C and a humidity of 60 %, the mechanical strength of PC, PCMF-1, PCMF-3, and MF composite aerogels was measured. By employing an electronic balance, vernier calipers, an electronic universal testing machine, a friction and wear testing machine, and a rheometer, the mass, volume, compressive modulus, friction coefficient, and creep compliance of the aerogels were respectively measured, thereby obtaining their apparent mechanical properties (Table S2), which encompassed density, compressive modulus, and specific modulus, among others.

Among these aerogels, the density of PC aerogel is as low as 10.76 ± 0.71 mg/cm3. In contrast, the densities of PCMF-1 and PCMF-3 are 97.14 ± 1.80 mg/cm3 and 101.33 ± 1.06 mg/cm3 respectively. Owing to its low solid content and the presence of a large number of hydrogen bonds within its molecular chains, the PC aerogel exhibits insufficient capillary stress strength during the freeze-drying process, resulting in a volume shrinkage as high as 24.6 %. However, upon the introduction of MF, the volume shrinkage rate of the PCMF-1 composite aerogel decreased to 15.1 %, which vividly demonstrates that MF can enhance the dimensional stability of the aerogel.

The compressive stress–strain curves (Fig. 1d) of PC, PCMF-1, and PCMF-3 composite aerogels display the typical deformation behavior characteristic of open-cell foams. In the low strain range, these aerogels exhibit linear elastic behavior, based on which the compressive modulus can be calculated. As the strain progresses to the medium level, the stress decreases due to the collapse of internal cells, subsequently entering the plastic yield plateau. Eventually, hardening occurs in the high strain range (with the end point of each compression test defined as when the sample reaches 90.0 % of its original height). Due to the brittle quality of MF aerogel, it was broken during the compression process, so the effective data could not be obtained. The PC aerogel is soft in texture, with its specific modulus (defined as the ratio of compressive modulus to density) being 2.43 ± 0.05 m2/s2 and its compressive modulus being 0.08 MPa. Notably, The mutual protection of PC and MF is helpful to improve the overall mechanical properties of PCMF composite aerogel. For instance, the specific modulus of the PCMF-3 composite aerogel can reach 40.86 ± 0.43 m2/s2, and its compressive modulus can reach 4.14 MPa, which are 16.81 times and 490.8 times those of the PC aerogel respectively.

The creep deformation and compliance tests were conducted on the above-mentioned composite aerogels (Fig. S1). The results indicated that under a constant external force of 3000 Pa, the strain of each aerogel increased over time. Specifically, in the initial stage, the strain grew relatively rapidly, followed by a gradual slowdown in the growth rate, and finally tended to stabilize in the later stage. The final strain value of the PC aerogel reached 3.34 %. This reveals that it is prone to deformation, has weak creep resistance, and high compliance. In contrast, the final strain value of the MF aerogel is merely 0.19 %. This indicates good creep resistance, low compliance, and high rigidity. The final strain values of PCMF-1 and PCMF-3 aerogels are 0.38 % and 0.84 % respectively, and both possess relatively low compliance and are significantly superior to that of the PC aerogel. Evidently, the introduction of MF substantially enhances the rigidity and creep resistance of the aerogel.

The friction coefficients of each aerogel were determined by utilizing a friction and wear testing machine (Fig. S2). Although certain fluctuations were observed during the testing period, the overall trend remained relatively stable. Due to its soft texture and smooth surface, the PC aerogel has a relatively low friction coefficient (0.2172 ± 0.0091). Conversely, the MF aerogel, with its relatively large rigidity and rough surface, exhibits a relatively high friction coefficient (0.6278 ± 0.0016), which often leads to significant wear and fragility issues in practical applications. The friction coefficient of PCMF-1 is reduced to 0.3886 ± 0.0026 after the flexible PC molecular chain is introduced into MF, thus improving its anti-friction performance. Similarly, with the further increase of PC content in MF, the friction coefficient of PCMF-3 is significantly reduced to 0.1697 ± 0.0015. In summary, the introduction of flexible PC molecular chains can significantly enhance the anti-friction performance of the aerogel, mitigate material wear, and enable it to strike a balance between rigidity and service life extension in practical applications.

On this basis, the basic structure of the PCMF composite aerogel can be described in the following manner: The molecular chain of PC aerogel is relatively flexible, the solid content is relatively low, and the number of crosslinking points is limited. The intermolecular interactions mainly rely on relatively weak hydrogen bonds, which leads to relatively large pore sizes of the PC aerogel [58,75]. Correspondingly, the specific modulus, compressive modulus, and creep resistance of the PC aerogel are at relatively low levels. Regarding MF, it is a random polymer. When the rigid MF is inserted between the layers of PC, an interpenetrati7ng cross-linked network is formed with PC, thereby providing support for PC. Meanwhile, it can reduce the pore size and pore wall thickness of the aerogel, thus optimizing its mechanical properties. Moreover, the addition of flexible PC molecular chains can protect MF, improve its friction performance, reduce wear, and, overall, enhance the aerogel's performance by balancing rigidity and extending the actual service life while reducing the occurrence of brittle fracture. Due to the synergistic effect of this unique microstructure, the mechanical properties of the composite aerogel have been significantly enhanced.

Thermal stability has a significant impact on the application of aerogel. TG-DSC was employed to analyze the composite aerogels of PC, MF, PCMF-1, and PCMF-3. A small peak at the beginning of all TGA curves is regarded as water loss (Fig. 1e). The initial decomposition temperature of PC is 210 °C. At 243 °C, the DTG curve exhibits the maximum peak value, indicating the fastest weight – loss rate (Fig. 1f), and there is no residue left at 600 °C. The peak temperature of the DTG curve for MF is higher (403 °C), which corresponds to the main decomposition of MF, with the release of formaldehyde, methanol, amines, and ammonia [76,77]. The final weight – loss stage beyond 435 °C is associated with the thermal degradation of melamine, and the final residual amount is 25.23 %, suggesting that MF has relatively good thermal stability. According to the characteristic thermal degradation data (Table 2 ), compared with PC aerogel, the Tdmax (the temperature at the maximum weight – loss rate) of PCMF-1 and PCMF-3aerogels increases to 387.39 °C and 370.04 °C, respectively. Although the decomposition temperatures of PCMF-1 and PCMF-3 aerogels are lower than that of MF, their weight loss rates have slowed down significantly. This might be attributed to the fact that the pyrolysis of PC leads to a gradual increase in the defects of the aerogel. As a consequence, decomposition reactions are more prone to occur at these structural defects, thereby resulting in advanced weight loss. However, a large number of long – chain PC molecules in the material are still wrapped around MF molecules through hydrogen bonding and other forces, restricting molecular movement and the rapid progress of decomposition reactions, thereby making the weight – loss rate more gradual. These results demonstrate that the interwoven and covalently cross – linked structure of PCMF aerogel enhances the thermal stability of the material.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical properties of PCMF aerogel and PCMF aerogel doped with luminescent molecules
ce:section.ce:para: Both PC and MF aerogels had a green afterglow and exhibit luminescence duration of 1 s and 1.5 s, respectively, when excited by a 300 nm UV lamp (Fig. 2 a). In order to better understand the emission characteristics of PC and MF aerogels, the photophysical properties of them were investigated using photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy. When excited at 300 nm, the transient PL spectrum of PC aerogel had multiple fluorescence emission peaks at 330–395 nm, while the delayed spectrum showed a green emission peak at 500 nm (Fig. 2b). The transient PL spectra of MF aerogel had fluorescence emission peaks at 318 nm, while the delayed spectra showed double emission peaks at 365 nm and 500 nm (Fig. 2c). The large Stokes shift, i.e., phosphorescence emission over 110 nm, confirms that the afterglow was a characteristic phosphorescence emission. The phosphorescence lifetime of PC and MF at 500 nm was 66.3 ms and 168.5 ms, respectively (Fig. 2d).

Since PC and MF had intrinsic room temperature phosphorescence, PCMF composite aerogels with undoped phosphorescent small molecules also had visible green phosphorescence and exhibit a luminescence duration of 3 s (Fig. 2a). Herein, we selected PCMF-3 aerogel to measure its photophysical properties. PL spectra show that PCMF-3 had a fluorescence emission peak at 330 nm and a phosphorescence emission peak at 490 nm. The afterglow decay profile shows that it had a lifetime of 194.9 ms (Fig. 2d, e). It’s postulated that the green phosphorescence emission of PCMF-3 aerogel is related to its rigid structure and CTE effect. CTE causes electron cloud sharing and conformation hardening, which enhances phosphorescence emission under environmental conditions. At the same time, this provides an excellent rigid substrate for the addition of phosphorescent small molecules. At the same time, this property provides an excellent rigid substrate for the addition of phosphorescent small molecules.

In order to obtain a long-lived RTP material, PCMF-3@PA was prepared by a simple physical blending of phenanthrene (PA) and PCMF-3. PCMF-3@PA had an ultra-long green afterglow of 20 s under ultraviolet lamp excitation (Fig. 3 a). When excited at 300 nm, the transient spectrum of PCMF-3@PA displayed multiple emission peaks in the range of 460–570 nm, which was very similar to the delayed spectra. The maximum fluorescence emission peak of the transient PL spectrum was 368 nm. After the fluorescence emission was filtered with a delay time of 0.2 ms, the delay spectra showed a maximum emission peak at 504 nm (Fig. 3b). The afterglow decay profile shows that PCMF-3@PA had a ultralong life of 1998.6 ms, meaning that a long-lived phosphorescent polymer aerogel was successfully prepared (Fig. 3c). PC@PA and MF@PA also showed a green afterglow. MF@PA exhibited a longer phosphorescence lifetime compared to PC@PA (Fig. S3), which further indicates that the rigid structure and the CTE effect enhance the phosphorescence emission.

As indicated in previous work [73], a series of colorful phosphorescent polymer aerogels was prepared, among which the red and yellow luminescence had phosphorescence lifetimes of 333.6 ms and 158.6 ms, respectively, indicating that phosphorescence emission in the long-wavelength range (greater than 600 nm) is difficult to achieve an ultra-long lifetime (greater than 1000 ms). Herein, by introducing R6G into PCMF-3@PA, we further explored the long-lived phosphorescence emission in the long-wavelength range based on the effective energy transfer process (FRET), resulting in the polymer aerogel PCMF-3@PG with an ultra-long yellow afterglow of 18 s (Fig. 3a). When excited at 300 nm, the transient spectrum of PCMF-3@PG shows the maximum emission peak at 571 nm, and the delayed spectrum shows emission peaks at 469 nm, 504 nm, and 571 nm (Fig. 3d). Compared with the emission peak of PCMF-3@PA at 542 nm, it can be seen that the corresponding emission peak of PCMF-3@PG had undergone a redshift, with a lifetime of 1294.8 ms at 571 nm (Fig. 3c). To confirm that the yellow luminescence of PCMF-3@PG comes from resonance energy transfer rather than Rhodamine 6G, a Rhodamine 6G-doped PCMF-3 aerogel (PCMF-3@R6G) was prepared. PCMF-3@R6G exhibited a luminescence duration of 4 s after the UV lamp was turned off (Fig. 3a). When excited at 300 nm, both the fluorescence and phosphorescence of PCMF-3@R6G were observed at 571 nm, and the afterglow decay profile shows a phosphorescence lifetime of 82.3 ms (Fig. 3e, 3f). This indicates that the long-lived yellow afterglow emission of PCMF-3@PG originates from FRET.

Subsequently, sulforhodamine 101 was introduced into PCMF-3@PG, successfully, obtaining the PCMF-3@PGS with red afterglow (Fig. 3a). The delayed spectrum of PCMF-3@PGS presented multiple emission peaks in the range of 460–620 nm, with the maximum emission peak at 620 nm (Fig. 3g). Compared with the emission peak of PCMF-3@PG at 571 nm, the corresponding emission peak of PCMF-3@PGS had experienced a redshift, and the lifetime at 620 nm is 1084.6 ms (Fig. 3c). Even though the delayed spectra of PCMF-3@SR101 and PCMF-3@PS have an emission peak at 620 nm, neither exhibit red afterglow, indicating that the red afterglow of PCMF-3@PGS originates from FRET (Fig. S4).

To verify the RTP characteristics, the phosphorescence emission spectra of PCMF-3 doped with luminescent molecules (PCMF-3@LM) were compared with those of PCMF-3. The RTP emission of PCMF-3@LM is significantly different from that of PCMF-3, indicating that their persistent room temperature phosphorescence (PRTP) is not caused by the intrinsic triplet state emission of the polymer PCMF-3. To determine whether the RTP properties of PCMF-3@LM originate from the intrinsic triplet state emission of the organic phosphor, their phosphorescence emission spectra were compared with those of organic phosphorescent material in a 2-MeTHF solution at 77 K [78]. The emission bands of PCMF-3@LM and their respective organic phosphorescent material almost overlap. Considering the changes in phosphorescence, it is suggested that the PRTP of PCMF-3@LM is attributed to the incorporation of the phosphorescent organic molecules and their interaction with the PCMF-3 network, which effectively restricts the vibrational rotation of the luminescent centers and inhibits the non-radiative transitions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Solvent sensitive properties of PCMF-3@PA aerogel
ce:section.ce:para: RTP materials can achieve stable phosphorescence emission in the air, but their RTP tends to disappear in humid solvent environment due to the disruption of hydrogen bonds. In our previous work [73], aerogel materials with excellent water resistance were constructed using polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) and MF as raw materials. In this work, to explore the impact of moisture on the phosphorescent properties of the aerogel, PVA was replaced with PC, which is more sensitive to the environment. The changes of luminescence intensity and duration in different solvents were studied with PCMF-3@PA as the sample, i.e., the sensitivity of phosphorescence to solvents.

The PCMF-3@PA aerogel (with dimension of 1 × 0.5 × 0.3 cm) was soaked in deionized water, and its luminescence was observed at regular intervals. It’s found that after soaking in water for 10 min, the phosphorescence did not immediately quench, but its intensity was reduced, and a 6 s afterglow duration could be observed (Fig. 4 ). Due to the hydrophobic effect in the MF covalent network, the stability of RTP material rises under humid condition. After soaking in water for 30 min, the phosphorescence of the aerogel was extremely weak, and it quenched after 60 min of soaking. Pectin itself is sensitive to water, preventing the aerogel from maintaining long-lasting phosphorescence in water. The PCMF-3@PA aerogel is highly sensitive to water, mainly for two reasons. First, the PCMF-3@PA aerogel used for solvent sensitivity studies has small dimensions and solid content, leading to faster water penetration into the interior and thus a faster rate of phosphorescence quenching. Second, the pectin has poor regularity, and the pure pectin aerogel doped with phenanthrene is not sufficient to form a dense rigid matrix for long-lasting phosphorescence emission (Fig. S3). This once again illustrates that MF plays a key role in the luminescence performance and water resistance of the aerogel. Furthermore, by combining pectin and MF, an aerogel that responds to the environment has been successfully obtained.

The PCMF-3@PA aerogel was immersed in seven organic solvents: Toluene (Tol), dichloromethane (DCM), 2-methoxyethanol (2-MeTHF), diethyl ether (Et2O), isopropyl alcohol (IPA), ethanol (EtOH), and ethyl acetate (EA). It’s found that even after soaking for 7 days, the aerogel could still emit a bright green afterglow (Fig. S5, Fig. 4d). Even when soaked in a solvent with high solubility and permeability like DCM, the aerogel maintained excellent afterglow. The experiments indicates that the aerogel had excellent chemical resistance and was not sensitive to a variety of organic solvents. The abovementioned results reflect that the polymer's rigid skeleton and dense network structure stabilize the triplet excitons, allowing the aerogel to have stable phosphorescence emission. The characteristic of maintaining stable phosphorescence emission in organic solvents provides possibilities for the application of phosphorescent materials in anti-counterfeiting and sensing in organic solvent environment.

However, when the aerogel is immersed in a relatively simple acetone solution, its phosphorescence intensity decreases rapidly (Fig. 4d). This is because the carbonyl group (CO) in the acetone molecule is a strong electron-withdrawing group. The carbonyl group may interfere with the energy state of the originally stable excited electrons by interacting with the luminescent center involved in phosphorescence generation within the aerogel, thereby preventing the electrons from being maintained at the appropriate energy level for phosphorescence generation and rapidly quenching the phosphorescence [79–81]. This conclusion was also confirmed by phosphorescence recovery after drying the samples (Fig. 4e). Subsequently, the aerogel was soaked in DMSO and DMF solutions respectively, and the phosphorescence was quenched after 30 min and 3 h (Fig. 4b, c). Neither DMSO nor DMF contains obvious electron-withdrawing groups; however, both possess certain hygroscopic properties [82]. Consequently, as the soaking time increases, the water content in the solvent gradually rises, leading to a gradual weakening of the phosphorescence. Moreover, the hygroscopicity of DMSO is significantly stronger than that of DMF, which results in a faster quenching of phosphorescence in the case of DMSO. This conclusion was also confirmed by phosphorescence recovery after drying the samples (Fig. 4e).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas sensitive properties of PCMF-3@PA aerogel
ce:section.ce:para: Through the aforementioned solvent sensitivity study on the PCMF-3@PA aerogel, it’s found that as-prepared aerogel was quite sensitive to water. The above phenomenon inspired us to explore the effects of water vapor on the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime of the aerogel. The PCMF-3@PA aerogel was placed in a plastic bottle cap inside a wide-mouth bottle containing 10 mL of deionized water, without touching the liquid. After sealing the wide-mouth bottle, the aerogel was exposed to water vapor for 10 min, and it still had a green afterglow exceeding 16 s, indicating that the humidity from the water vapor was not enough to quench the phosphorescence of the aerogel in a short time (Fig. 5 a). After continuous exposure to water vapor for 5 h, the phosphorescence intensity became very weak, and the phosphorescence quenched after 7 h.

The aerogel can maintain luminescence in the aforementioned organic solvents, and it can be inferred that it can also possess stable phosphorescence emission in the vapors of these organic solvents. To further investigate the gas sensitivity of the phosphorescent aerogel, experiments with both alkaline and acidic gases were conducted. PCMF-3@PA was exposed to wide-mouth bottles containing ammonia water and acetic acid, respectively. After 10 min, it was removed and no significant changes in luminescence intensity and lifetime were observed (Fig. 5b, c). However, after exposure for 7 h, the phosphorescence quenched.

The PCMF-3@PA was exposed to vapors form formic acid solution in a wide-mouth bottle, and it was taken out at regular intervals. By observing the afterglow of the aerogel after the UV light was turned off, it’s found that as the exposure time to the formic acid vapor increased, the phosphorescence intensity on its surface gradually decreased, while the interior maintained its original phosphorescence (Fig. 6 c). Reason for this is that the aerogel surface adsorbs the formic acid vapor first, leading to the quenching of phosphorescence, while the interior has not yet adsorbed enough formic acid. After being exposed to formic acid vapor for 10 min, although the afterglow of the aerogel can be captured in electronic photographs, it is almost imperceptible to naked eyes. The phenomenon of phosphorescence change caused by gas adsorption, as described above, provides a broad application prospect for the study of room temperature phosphorescent materials in the field of sensors.

Photophysical measurements of the aerogel exposed to formic acid for varying time periods were conducted. The phosphorescence spectra revealed that the aerogel's phosphorescence intensity decreased with increasing exposure time. After 3 min of exposure to formic acid vapor, the intensity dropped by 96 %, and after 10 min, the phosphorescence lifetime decreased from 1925.0 ms to 47.9 ms (Fig. 6a, b). This is because formic acid is volatile and can quickly diffuse and penetrate the aerogel. Its acidity enables the dissociation of hydrogen ions (H+). Even in vapor form near the aerogel's surface, these ions can affect the microenvironment's pH around the luminescent centers, altering their electronic structures. In addition, hydrogen ions may have coordination effects with atoms with lone pair electrons in the luminous center, destroying the stability of the electron states required by phosphorescence, and rapidly quenching phosphorescence [83–86]. This conclusion was also confirmed by phosphorescence recovery after drying the samples (Fig. 6d). The aerogel showed different behaviors in formic acid and acetic acid vapors, quenching more rapidly in formic acid vapor. Although acetic acid vapor has some volatility, its larger molecular structure and higher steric hindrance reduce its diffusion efficiency into the aerogel's pores to contact luminescent centers. Besides, being less acidic than formic acid, it has a lower tendency to dissociate hydrogen ions in the vapor state and limited ability to change the electronic structure of the luminescence center, thus failing to quickly disrupt phosphorescence generation conditions and unable to quickly quench the phosphorescence.

In order to determine whether the RTP response of the PCMF-3@PA aerogel under formic acid vapor stimulation exhibits cyclic reversibility, the phosphorescence signals of the PCMF-3@PA aerogel after being fumigated with formic acid vapor for 10 min and that of the PCMF-3@PA aerogel after drying at 60 °C for 3 h were measured. As depicted in Fig. 6d, the drying/fumigation process was repeated five times. It was observed that even though the film was fumigated by formic acid vapor each time, the phosphorescence signal would almost disappear. However, once the aerogel was dried at 60 °C for 5 h, the phosphorescence signal could largely recover and remain relatively stable.

In this study, a long-lived phosphorescent composite aerogel with unique properties was successfully fabricated, and its performance advantages stem from the ingenious structural design. During the construction of the PCMF aerogel, PC and MF were combined in specific proportions and underwent steps such as covalent cross-linking, freeze-drying, and aging to form an ordered interpenetrating structure. The interaction between PC and MF plays a crucial role in optimizing the material's properties. Thanks to its rigid structure and high mechanical strength, MF enhances the dimensional stability and rigidity of the aerogel, reduces the volume shrinkage rate of the PC aerogel caused by its own characteristics, and improves mechanical properties such as the compressive modulus. In contrast, the flexibility of PC endows the aerogel with good flexibility, improves the friction performance, and reduces the wear problems brought about by the rigidity of MF, enabling the material to better balance rigidity and service life in practical applications. After doping the PCMF-3 aerogel with phenanthrene, due to the excellent structural properties of the aerogel, non-radiative transitions were effectively inhibited, achieving an ultra-long phosphorescence lifetime of 1.9 s. Based on the principle of resonance energy transfer, introducing R6G and SR101 obtained long-wavelength and long-afterglow effects, expanding the luminescence characteristics of phosphorescent materials. This material exhibits a specific response to formic acid gas, resulting in a significant decrease in phosphorescence intensity within 10 min, while no such obvious phenomenon occurs in other common gases. Moreover, the RTP response of the aerogel has a reversible cycle characteristic, which provides a highly sensitive and selective new approach for the detection of harmful gases in environmental monitoring. It has great application potential in the field of environmental monitoring and vigorously promotes the development of phosphorescent materials in this field.
